mnemonics: NEW DEAL: New/Delete Enhanced for Allocation & Lifetime (highlights custom memory management)
INHERIT THE MEMORY: Overloaded operators are not inherited, you must redefine them in derived classes.
SCOPE YOUR OPERATORS: Global vs. Class scope for new/delete overloading determines their application.
keyPoints: Overloading `new` and `delete` allows you to customize dynamic memory management for objects of a specific class, enabling techniques like using custom memory pools or adding debug tracking.
When overloading, you must provide both the single-object (`new`/`delete`) and the array versions (`new[]`/`delete[]`) for complete control and to prevent mismatched operations.

Overloaded `new` and `delete` operators are static member functions by default (they do not have a `this` pointer) and are not automatically inherited by derived classes; they must be explicitly redefined if needed.
commonMistakes: Mismatching the overloaded `new` with the standard `delete` (or vice-versa), or overloading `new` but not `delete`, which leads to undefined behavior and memory leaks.
Forgetting to overload the array versions (`new[]` and `delete[]`), causing the compiler to use the global versions when allocating arrays of objects, which breaks custom management for those arrays.

Assuming that overloading `new` and `delete` for a base class automatically applies to derived classes, which it does not; the operators must be redefined for each derived class that requires custom allocation.
